general.normal: Your {test_name} is within normal range, which is good news.
general.slight_abnormal: Your {test_name} is slightly {direction}. Worth monitoring but no need to worry excessively.
general.moderate_abnormal: Your {test_name} is moderately {direction}. Consulting a doctor for further evaluation is recommended.
general.significant_abnormal: Your {test_name} is significantly {direction}. Seeking medical attention soon is recommended.
general.critical: Your {test_name} is at a critical level. Please seek immediate medical attention!

low.mild: Mildly decreased white blood cells, possibly caused by viral infection, medication effects, or mild nutritional deficiency.
low.moderate: Significantly decreased white blood cells; immunity may be reduced, increasing infection risk. Follow up and consult a doctor.
low.severe: Severely decreased white blood cells; high infection risk requiring medical intervention.

high.mild: Mildly elevated white blood cells, may indicate minor infection, stress, or post-exercise state.
high.moderate: Significantly elevated white blood cells, may indicate bacterial infection, inflammation, or tissue damage.
high.severe: Severely elevated white blood cells, may indicate serious infection or leukemia. Urgent evaluation needed.

low.mild: Mild anemia; slight fatigue possible. Increase iron-rich foods in diet.
low.moderate: Moderate anemia; symptoms such as dizziness and fatigue may occur. Identify cause and treat.
low.severe: Severe anemia requiring immediate medical intervention; transfusion may be needed.

high.mild: Mildly elevated hemoglobin, possibly due to dehydration or high-altitude environment. Drink more water.
high.moderate: Significantly elevated hemoglobin; polycythemia or other conditions may be present.
high.severe: Severely elevated hemoglobin; increased blood viscosity with high thrombosis risk.

low.mild: Mildly decreased platelets; coagulation essentially normal. Avoid trauma.
low.moderate: Significantly decreased platelets; increased bleeding risk. Avoid strenuous exercise and injury.
low.severe: Severely decreased platelets; high bleeding risk requiring medical intervention.

high.mild: Mildly elevated platelets; generally no special treatment needed.
high.moderate: Significantly elevated platelets; increased thrombosis risk. Further examination recommended.
high.severe: Severely elevated platelets; very high thrombosis risk requiring treatment.

high.mild: Total cholesterol slightly elevated; borderline high. Adjust diet and increase exercise.
high.moderate: Total cholesterol significantly elevated; increased cardiovascular disease risk. Low-fat diet and follow-up recommended.
high.severe: Total cholesterol severely elevated; medication treatment and lifestyle changes required.

high.mild: LDL (bad cholesterol) mildly elevated; reduce saturated fat intake.
high.moderate: LDL significantly elevated; increased atherosclerosis risk.
high.severe: LDL severely elevated; medication treatment strongly recommended.

low.mild: HDL (good cholesterol) slightly low; increase aerobic exercise.
low.moderate: HDL significantly low; cardiovascular protective effect reduced.
low.severe: HDL severely insufficient; increased cardiovascular risk.

high.mild: Triglycerides mildly elevated; reduce sugar and refined carbohydrates.
high.moderate: Triglycerides significantly elevated; increased pancreatitis risk. Limit alcohol.
high.severe: Triglycerides severely elevated; high acute pancreatitis risk requiring medication.

high.mild: ALT mildly elevated; may be caused by fatty liver, alcohol, or medications.
high.moderate: ALT significantly elevated; indicates hepatocyte damage. Liver function tests and ultrasound recommended.
high.severe: ALT severely elevated; acute hepatitis or other serious liver disease may be present.

high.mild: AST mildly elevated; may be caused by muscle damage or mild liver disease.
high.moderate: AST significantly elevated; differentiate between liver or myocardial damage.
high.severe: AST severely elevated; serious liver damage or myocardial infarction may be present.

low.mild: Creatinine mildly low; may be due to low muscle mass or insufficient protein intake.

high.mild: Creatinine mildly elevated; may indicate mild decline in kidney function.
high.moderate: Creatinine significantly elevated; kidney function impaired. Nephrology evaluation needed.
high.severe: Creatinine severely elevated; kidney failure may be present. Urgent treatment needed.

high.mild: Uric acid mildly elevated; drink more water, reduce high-purine food intake.
high.moderate: Uric acid significantly elevated; increased gout risk. Follow low-purine diet.
high.severe: Uric acid severely elevated; high risk of gout and kidney stones. Medication treatment needed.

low.mild: Blood glucose mildly low; may be due to prolonged fasting. Eat appropriately.
low.moderate: Blood glucose significantly low; hypoglycemia symptoms may occur. Carry candy with you.
low.severe: Blood glucose severely low; immediate sugar supplementation needed.

high.mild: Fasting blood glucose mildly elevated; prediabetes stage. Diet control and exercise recommended.
high.moderate: Fasting blood glucose significantly elevated; diabetes possible. Further glucose tolerance testing recommended.
high.severe: Fasting blood glucose severely elevated; diabetes confirmed. Standardized treatment required.

high.mild: HbA1c mildly elevated; blood glucose control over the past 3 months was average.
high.moderate: HbA1c significantly elevated; recent blood glucose control poor. Treatment plan adjustment needed.
high.severe: HbA1c severely elevated; long-term poor blood glucose control with high complication risk.

low.mild: TSH mildly decreased; may indicate subclinical hyperthyroidism.
low.moderate: TSH significantly decreased; hyperthyroidism possible. Thyroid hormone testing recommended.
low.severe: TSH severely decreased; hyperthyroidism highly likely.

high.mild: TSH mildly elevated; may indicate subclinical hypothyroidism.
high.moderate: TSH significantly elevated; hypothyroidism possible. Thyroid hormone supplementation may be needed.
high.severe: TSH severely elevated; hypothyroidism highly likely. Treatment required.

high.mild: CRP mildly elevated; minor inflammation may be present.
high.moderate: CRP significantly elevated; inflammation or infection present.
high.severe: CRP severely elevated; serious infection or autoimmune disease may be present.

general.diet: Maintain a balanced diet; eat more vegetables and fruits; reduce processed foods.
general.exercise: Get at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise per week.
general.lifestyle: Maintain regular sleep schedule; avoid staying up late; quit smoking and limit alcohol.
general.followup: Regular follow-up testing is recommended to monitor trends in indicators.
general.medical: Consulting a professional doctor for a personalized treatment plan is recommended.

blood_routine.low_wbc: Maintain personal hygiene; avoid crowded places; prevent colds.
blood_routine.high_wbc: Get plenty of rest, drink water, and seek medical attention promptly if fever develops.
blood_routine.low_hgb: Eat more iron-rich foods such as lean meat, animal liver, and spinach.
blood_routine.low_plt: Avoid strenuous exercise; use a soft-bristle toothbrush; prevent injury.

lipid.high_ldl: Reduce animal fat; choose olive oil; eat more deep-sea fish.
lipid.high_tg: Limit alcohol; reduce refined sugars and sweets; control weight.
lipid.low_hdl: Increase aerobic exercise; quit smoking; moderate red wine consumption (if you already drink).

liver.high_alt: Abstain from alcohol; avoid hepatotoxic medications; recheck liver function regularly.
liver.fatty_liver: Control weight; follow a low-fat diet; increase exercise.

kidney.high_crea: Low-salt, low-protein diet; control blood pressure and blood glucose; avoid nephrotoxic medications.
kidney.high_ua: Drink plenty of water (over 2000ml daily); reduce seafood and organ meat intake.

diabetes.high_glucose: Control staple food portions; choose low glycemic index foods; walk after meals.
diabetes.monitor: Monitor blood glucose regularly; learn self-management of blood glucose.
